items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: In Loftus & Palmer, which verb produced a mean estimate of 34.0 mph?
items.why: Correct answer: Hit. “Smashed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In Gabbert et al. 2003 what percent of discussers reported details they had not seen?
items.why: Correct answer: 71%. “23%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Mean speed estimate for the 'smashed' condition in Loftus & Palmer 1974 (mph) was approximately what?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
